findings.summary: ManuscriptMetrics defined with different fields in two separate modules, causing name collision for callers
evidence: infrastructure/llm/review/metrics.py:28 — @dataclass class ManuscriptMetrics with fields: total_chars, total_words, total_tokens_est, truncated, truncated_chars
infrastructure/reporting/executive_reporter.py:25 — @dataclass class ManuscriptMetrics with fields: sections, total_words, total_lines, markdown_files, equations, figures, references
infrastructure/llm/review/generator.py:54 — imports ManuscriptMetrics from infrastructure.llm.review.metrics
Both classes share the name ManuscriptMetrics but represent completely different domain concepts (LLM input truncation vs. manuscript content statistics)
findings.suggestion: Rename one of the two classes to eliminate the collision: the LLM-side class should be LLMManuscriptMetrics or ManuscriptInputMetrics; the reporting-side class can keep ManuscriptMetrics. Update all callers accordingly.

findings.summary: ScanResults and AccuracyIssue/CompletenessGap defined independently in both doc_models.py and repo_scanner.py with differing field sets
evidence: infrastructure/validation/doc_models.py:43 — AccuracyIssue with fields: file, line, issue_type, issue_message, severity
infrastructure/validation/repo_scanner.py:30 — AccuracyIssue with fields: category, severity, file, line, message, details — different field names for same concept
infrastructure/validation/doc_models.py:53 — CompletenessGap with fields: category, item, description, severity
infrastructure/validation/repo_scanner.py:41 — CompletenessGap with identical fields but defined again locally
infrastructure/validation/doc_models.py:74 — ScanResults dataclass; infrastructure/validation/repo_scanner.py:51 — separate ScanResults dataclass with different fields
repo_scanner.py does not import from doc_models.py despite duplicating its domain vocabulary
findings.suggestion: Consolidate AccuracyIssue, CompletenessGap, and ScanResults into doc_models.py with a unified field schema. Extend AccuracyIssue with a 'category' field and an optional 'details' field so both scanners can use one type. Have repo_scanner.py import from doc_models.py.

findings.summary: doc_scanner.py re-exports all doc_models symbols via __all__, obscuring that doc_models.py is the canonical owner
evidence: infrastructure/validation/doc_scanner.py:37-45 — __all__ lists AccuracyIssue, CompletenessGap, DocumentationFile, LinkIssue, QualityIssue, ScanResults alongside DocumentationScanner
infrastructure/validation/doc_scanner.py:36 — comment '# Re-export doc_models symbols so callers can import them from this module' — explicitly acknowledging the re-export pattern
This means callers can import ScanResults from either doc_scanner or doc_models, creating two canonical import paths for one type
findings.suggestion: Remove the re-export block from doc_scanner.py. Callers needing scan result types should import directly from doc_models. doc_scanner.__all__ should contain only DocumentationScanner.

findings.summary: Production fragment dispatch in PromptComposer checks for the literal string '#test_template' to change behavior
evidence: infrastructure/llm/prompts/composer.py:72 — if fragment_ref.endswith('#test_template'): — hardcoded test-specific string in the production dispatch chain
infrastructure/llm/prompts/composer.py:74-75 — Special handling for section structures triggered only when the fragment ref ends with this test sentinel
The comment on line 73 says 'Special handling for section structures' but the condition is checking a test-specific name, not a semantic property of the fragment type
findings.suggestion: Replace the string-sentinel dispatch with a structural check: inspect the fragment type or use a dedicated fragment key (e.g., 'type': 'section_structure' in the template JSON). Never use test artifact names as production routing keys.

findings.summary: Each _build_* method in PromptComposer maintains an inline fallback string that duplicates the content of its corresponding JSON fragment file
evidence:
infrastructure/llm/prompts/composer.py:197-199 — _build_format_requirements fallback: hardcoded string 'FORMAT REQUIREMENTS:

1. Use markdown
2. Headers:
${headers_list}'
infrastructure/llm/prompts/composer.py:243-245 — _build_content_requirements fallback: hardcoded full content requirements string duplicating the JSON file content
infrastructure/llm/prompts/composer.py:330-335 — _build_token_budget_awareness fallback: hardcoded token budget format string
infrastructure/llm/prompts/composer.py:378-381 — _build_validation_hints fallback: hardcoded validation format string
Four separate fallbacks each duplicate the logic of their respective JSON files; if the format changes in the JSON, the Python fallback silently diverges
findings.suggestion: Remove the inline fallback strings. If the fragment file is missing, raise LLMTemplateError immediately so the misconfiguration is detected rather than silently falling back to stale hardcoded content. The JSON files are the single source of truth.

findings.summary: Production completeness logic in repo_scanner.py and doc_scanner.py special-cases 'comprehensive_doc_scan.py' by name to skip it
evidence: infrastructure/validation/repo_scanner.py:371 — if script.name not in documented_scripts and script.name != 'comprehensive_doc_scan.py':
infrastructure/validation/repo_scanner.py:476 — if script.name.startswith('_') or script.name == 'comprehensive_doc_scan.py': continue
The production scan logic uses a hardcoded specific filename as a bypass condition rather than a general mechanism (e.g., a naming convention like leading underscore, or an exclusion list in config)
findings.suggestion: Use a general exclusion mechanism: either a configurable exclude list passed to the scanner, or adopt the existing leading-underscore convention already used on line 476 (startswith('_')) and rename the file accordingly. Remove the hardcoded name from both locations.

findings.summary: total_stage_time is computed in _format_text_summary but only used inside the following if-block; the variable is unnecessary
evidence: infrastructure/core/pipeline_summary.py:127-128 — executed_stages computed, then total_stage_time = sum(r.duration for r in executed_stages)
infrastructure/core/pipeline_summary.py:141 — avg_time = total_stage_time / len(executed_stages) — only use of total_stage_time

In _format_html_summary (line 299-301) the equivalent computation is done inline without an intermediate variable: sum(r.duration for r in executed_stages) / len(executed_stages) — inconsistent pattern
findings.suggestion: Inline the sum into the avg_time computation on line 141: avg_time = sum(r.duration for r in executed_stages) / len(executed_stages). This matches the HTML formatter and eliminates the redundant binding.

findings.summary: The display_order category list is copy-pasted identically into both _generate_text_report and _generate_html_report in FileInventoryManager
evidence: infrastructure/core/file_inventory.py:203-214 — display_order = ['pdf', 'figures', 'data', 'reports', 'simulations', 'llm', 'logs', 'web', 'slides', 'tex'] in _generate_text_report
infrastructure/core/file_inventory.py:309-320 — identical display_order list in _generate_html_report
The same 10-element list also appears implicitly in OUTPUT_CATEGORIES at line 39-50, creating three representations of the same ordered set
findings.suggestion: Extract the display order as a class-level constant (e.g., DISPLAY_ORDER) and reference it in both format methods and in OUTPUT_CATEGORIES. A single definition eliminates the copy-paste maintenance risk.

findings.summary: Branches that contribute zero to a running score counter explicitly write 'score += 0', adding noise with no effect
evidence: infrastructure/reporting/executive_reporter.py:569 — score += 0 (poor coverage branch)
infrastructure/reporting/executive_reporter.py:602 — score += 0 (high failure rate branch)
infrastructure/reporting/executive_reporter.py:639 — score += 0 (insufficient manuscript branch)
infrastructure/reporting/executive_reporter.py:676 — score += 0 (limited outputs branch)
Four separate occurrences; the pattern creates false symmetry with meaningful score increments
findings.suggestion: Remove all 'score += 0' statements. The else-branches that assign zero contribute nothing and should contain only the factors dict assignment.

findings.summary: PipelineSummaryGenerator has three nearly identical format methods (_format_text_summary, _format_json_summary, _format_html_summary) each independently extracting and computing the same fields from PipelineSummary
evidence: infrastructure/core/pipeline_summary.py:127-128 — text formatter computes executed_stages and total_stage_time independently
infrastructure/core/pipeline_summary.py:299-301 — html formatter recomputes executed_stages and average inline
infrastructure/core/pipeline_summary.py:213-222 — json formatter re-walks stage_results independently, extracting the same stage fields
The log_file final-path computation (_get_final_log_path) is called separately in all three formatters at lines 118, 247, 266 — three callers of identical logic on the same field
No shared normalization step prepares a display-ready struct; each formatter is a full traversal
findings.suggestion: Extract a _normalize_summary_for_display(summary) method that computes executed_stages, avg_time, slowest_pct, final_log_path, and other derived values once. Pass the normalized struct to each format method, which then only handle rendering.

findings.summary: FileInventoryManager.generate_inventory_report and PipelineSummaryGenerator.format_summary both dispatch on a format string, but use different parameter names ('output_format' vs 'format') and different default values, solving the same problem differently
evidence: infrastructure/core/file_inventory.py:158 — def generate_inventory_report(self, entries, output_format='text', base_dir=None)
infrastructure/core/pipeline_summary.py:82 — def format_summary(self, summary, format='text')
Same concept (output format selection with string dispatch) uses different parameter names in sibling infrastructure modules; 'format' shadows the builtin in pipeline_summary.py
findings.suggestion: Standardize the parameter name to 'output_format' across all format-dispatch functions in the infrastructure layer. This also avoids shadowing the Python builtin 'format'.

findings.summary: multi_project_reporter.py contains two top-level report functions (generate_multi_project_report and generate_multi_project_summary_report) that solve the same multi-project summary concern with overlapping but inconsistent data models
evidence: infrastructure/reporting/multi_project_reporter.py:28 — generate_multi_project_report takes repo_root + project_names (strings), delegates to executive_reporter

infrastructure/reporting/multi_project_reporter.py:70 — generate_multi_project_summary_report takes MultiProjectResult + project objects, builds its own summary dict with overlapping fields (total_projects, successful_projects, failed_projects, total_duration)

Both functions produce multi-project summary reports but construct independent data models with no shared normalization; the performance_analysis block in generate_multi_project_summary_report (lines 119-137) reimplements cross-project aggregation that executive_reporter.py already provides via generate_aggregate_metrics
findings.suggestion: Merge the two functions into one entrypoint that accepts a MultiProjectResult and delegates exclusively to executive_reporter for all aggregation. Remove the ad-hoc summary dict construction in generate_multi_project_summary_report and replace it with a call to generate_executive_summary / collect_project_metrics.
